coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive K+ fluorescence/absorption di-mode assay based on highly co-catalysiscarbon dot nanozyme and DNAzyme
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chongning
Liu, Qiwen
Wang, Xiyin
Luo, Yanghe
Jiang, Zhiliang
coredata.dc:description: Doped carbon dots (CD) exhibit novel luminescence properties that was commonly used as fluorescence probesinnanoanalysis. The another excellent property of catalysis was also interesting to analysts to amplify signal in spectral assay. In this article, highly catalysis N,Au co-doped carbon dots (CDN/Au) were quickly fabricated by microwave irradiation, using corn starch, ethylenediamineand chloroauric acid as precursors. Some preparedCDs were characterized in details by molecular spectroscopy, diffraction of X-ray (XRD) and Michaelis constant. The CDN/Aushowedpowerful catalysis in the fluorescence/absorption indicator reaction of H2O2-3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to from oxidized productTMBOXwith fluorescencepeak at 403 nm and absorption peak at 563 nm. The nanoenzyme reaction was coupled with the DNAzyme reaction of K+-aptamer(Apt)-hemin (HM), a new dienzymecatalytic fluorescence/absorption di-modemethod for ultratrace K+ was developed. In addition, the analytical principle and the dienzyme co-catalysis mechanism were studied.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,Au-doped carbon dots
Nanozyme
K+
DNAzyme
Fluorescence
Di-mode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence and absorption spectra of nanocatalytic system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Apt mediated CDN/Au and DNAzymedicatalyzed fluorescence/absorption detection of ultratrace K+.

Excitation, fluorescence, absorption and IR spectraof CDN1/Au2. A: EX spectra, 2.3 × 10−4 mol/L Tris-HCl, the a to gcurves represent 0, 0.05, 0.09, 0.36, 0.55, 0.73 and 1.09 mg/mL CDN1/Au2 respectively. B:FL spectra, a:2.3 × 10−4 mol/L Tris-HCl, the a to g curves represent 0, 0.05, 0.09, 0.36, 0.55, 0.73 and 1.09 mg/mL CDN1/Au2respectively. C: absorption spectra, 2.3 × 10−4 mol/L Tris-HCl, the a to g curves represent 0, 0.05, 0.09, 0.36, 0.55, 0.73 and 1.09 mg/mL CDN1/Au2 respectively. (D) IR of CDN1/Au2.
XRDandTEM. (A) XRD of CDN, (B) XRD ofCDN1/Au2, (C) TEM of CDN, (D) TEM of CDN1Au2.

Fluorescence spectra of nanocatalysis system. A:CDN/Au1-H2O2-TMB system, the a to h curves represent 0, 1.3, 2.7, 6.3, 12.5, 18.8, 25 and 31.3 µg/mL CD N/Au1 respectively. B: CDN1-H2O2-TMB system, the a to g curves represent 0, 0.18, 0.46, 0.73, 1.09, 1.46 and 1.82 µg/mL CDN1 respectively. C:CDN1/Au2-H2O2-TMB system, the a to g curves represent 0, 0.02, 0.05 ng/mL, 0.07, 0.09, 0.14 and 0.23 µg/mL CDN1/Au2 respectively.D: CDN1-H2O2-TMB-HM-Apt-K+ system, the a to g curves represent 0, 1 × 10−8, 3 × 10−8 mol/L, 5 × 10−8, 7 × 10−8, 10 × 10−8, 13 × 10−8 mol/L K+ respectively. E: CDN/Au1-H2O2-TMB-HM-Apt-K+ system, the a to h curves represent 0, 1 × 10−8, 8 × 10−8, 13 × 10−8, 17 × 10−8, 23 × 10−8 and 27 × 10−8 mol/L K+ respectively. F:CDN1/Au2-H2O2-TMB-HM-Apt-K+ system, the a to h curves represent 0, 6 × 10−11, 3 × 10−10, 5 × 10−10, 7 × 10−10, 1 × 10−9 and 1.3 × 10−9 mol/L K+ respectively.
CDN/Au nanozyme and DNAzyme co-catalysis mechanism.

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive K+ fluorescence/absorption di-mode assay based on highly co-catalysiscarbon dot nanozyme and DNAzyme

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University), Ministry of Education, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin 541004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Bioengineering, Hezhou University, Hezhou 542899, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Bioengineering, Hezhou University, Hezhou 542899, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University)
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University), Ministry of Education, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin 541004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Guangxi Normal University, Guilin, 541004, China (Z. Jiang).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doped carbon dots (CD) exhibit novel luminescence properties that was commonly used as fluorescence probesinnanoanalysis. The another excellent property of catalysis was also interesting to analysts to amplify signal in spectral assay. In this article, highly catalysis N,Au co-doped carbon dots (CDN/Au) were quickly fabricated by microwave irradiation, using corn starch, ethylenediamineand chloroauric acid as precursors. Some preparedCDs were characterized in details by molecular spectroscopy, diffraction of X-ray (XRD) and Michaelis constant. The CDN/Aushowedpowerful catalysis in the fluorescence/absorption indicator reaction of H2O2-3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to from oxidized productTMBOXwith fluorescencepeak at 403 nm and absorption peak at 563 nm. The nanoenzyme reaction was coupled with the DNAzyme reaction of K+-aptamer(Apt)-hemin (HM), a new dienzymecatalytic fluorescence/absorption di-modemethod for ultratrace K+ was developed. In addition, the analytical principle and the dienzyme co-catalysis mechanism were studied.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-based fluorescencenanomaterials with the characteristics of low toxicity, good biocompatibility and water solubility, stable photoluminescence and colloidal properties. Thesimpleand environment friendly preparation of CDs withcost-low raw materials has attracted the attention of many researchers [1–3]. Currently, most of the procedures for preparing high luminescence CDs with chemicals and natural plants as precursors include hydrothermal, microwave radiation, laser ablation, and chemical oxidation methods [4–7]. The hydrothermal is classic and commonprocedure. It had been applied to the preparation of fluorescent CDs using inexpensive biomass waste as a carbon source [8], and Fe3+ could quench the fluorescence to detect 0.2–100 μmol/LFe3+. Doping is an important method to improve the properties of CDs. The doping of non-metallic and metallic impurities into CDs can effectively adjust its energy gap and electron density, enhance its chemical activity, and further improve its application performance. Wang et al [9] prepared a new type of N-doped CDsby solid heating method using glycine and urea as raw materials with fluorescence. It was used as a fluorescent probe to detect 0.0972 μmol/L dopamine. Chikkili et al. [10] useda solid-state sensing platform by synthetic P-dopedCDs, and 0.27 nmol/L Cu2+can be detected by electroluminescence (ELC) using H2O2 as a co-reactant. Fan et al. [11] used 1-(2-pyridylazo)-2-naohthalenol and Mn2+ as precursors to prepare Mn-doped CDswithhydrothermal procedure, and used for fluorescence detection of 0.1–200 μmol/L trigeminal neurotransmitter. Co-doping is one of the methods to improve the quantum yield. Lin et al. [12] exploited a fluorescent N, S co-doped CDprobe for detecting 0.4–41.3 μg/mL methotrexatebased on the fluorescence internal filtering effect. Zhao et al. [13] composited the N,F co-doped carbon dotsby hydrothermal method, which was used to detect 12 nmol/L ClO. Guo et al. [14] prepared S,N co-doped carbon dotswith hydrothermal methodand studied their effects on the reaction by the Luminol-Mn(IV) Cl, and utilized for the determination to0.05–1.5 μmol/L uric acid. The microwave procedure was environment friendly, simple and fast [15,16]. Salinas-Castillo et al. [15] reported a one-step microwave to prepare water soluble doped N-CDs to sensoring Cu2+ ions, using citric acid andpolyethylenimineas precusor. Li et al. [16].proposed a microwave-assisted synthesis procedure to prepare N, S co-doped CDs as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for 0.01–70 mg/L. At present, most reported CD analysis methods were based on redox and coordination reactions, and their selectivity and sensitivity need to be improved by means of new highly selective bioanalytical reaction such as aptamer (Apt) and strongly nanocatalytic amplification.

Apt has been used as a recognition component in biosensing to detect analytes, including ion, small molecule and protein. Compared to protein antibodies, ithas many advantages such as ease of chemical synthesis, low production cost, high stability, chemical modifiable, and specificity adjustable [17–20]. Ji et al. [21] used thioflavin Tas a fluorescent indicator to develop a method of fluorescent for label-free detection to 0.1–1000 μmol/L ATP based on aptamer mapping. Fan et al. [22] reported a CDs-Apt colorimetric biosensor for detection of 20 nmol/LPBP2a. Wang et al. [23] used adenosineApt to modify the CDs to prepare anApt transducer for thedetection 0.63 nmol/L AD. Zhu et al. [24] studied a biosensor of fluorescent based on Apt-CDs and nanographitetothe detection of 0.10–5.00 nmol/Ladenosine. Wang et al. [25] synthesized a fluorescent N-CDs and assembled them on Apt-AuNPsfor hypersensitive detection of 5 pg/mL–2.00 ng/mL aflatoxin B1 (AFB1). In the labeled aptamer analysis, it is necessary to label the Apt, which on the one hand will weaken the biological activity, affect the affinity and conformational changes between the Apt and target molecules, resulting in decreased selectivity and sensitivity. On the other hand, the labeledmakes the operation cumbersome and expensive. In contrast, Apt based non-labeling analysis is characterized by low cost, simple operation and sensitivity, which makes the non-labeling method more and more attention, especially combined with DNAzyme. It has been used for spectrophotometric and fluorescence detection of trace amounts of Pb2+ and adenosine, based on label-free Apt DNAzyme [26]. Catalytic amplification is one of the important ways to improve the sensitivity of analytical method. Enzyme catalysis has strong specificity but low stability and is easy to be inactivated, metal nanoparticles have strong catalysis but are easy to be aggregated by salt and unstable. Both CD nanozyme and DNAzyme with excellent stability and catalysis, overcome the above shortcomings. Therefore, it is a new approach for the application of CDs in nanoanalysis to explore the preparation of stable and highly catalytic codoped CDs and their catalytic fluorescence indication reactions, and to apply them to non-labeled Apt DNAzyme catalytic fluorescence analysis.

TMB is commonly used non-toxic color reagent, which can be used for enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and inorganic determination by photometry [27]. The MnO2nanoreactioncan form yellow compounds by oxidizing TMB, which has an absorption peak at 450 nm, it can be used for photometric determination of 0.3 μmol/L Fe(II) [28]. Based on thehorseradish peroxidase (HRP) catalytic amplification, TMB has also been applied in photometric analysis [29]. Ag+ can oxidize TMB to form the oxidized TMB (TMBOX) withblue color, and 0.010 U/mLtyrosinase (TYR) can be determined photometrically [30]. Cu2+ catalyzes the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 to produce a blue product and a colorimetric sensing platformwas reported for 1–100 μmol/L Cu2+ [31]. However, there has been no report that TMBOX is used as a fluorescent probe and coupled with co-doped CDs and DNAzymecatalytic amplifications to detect K+ by dual mode fluorescence/absorption method.

Potassium ion is an important element that affects the structure of the human heart and neuromuscular function, with important physiological functions for living organisms. Methods for detecting K+ include photometry, atomic absorption spectroscopy, resonance scattering spectroscopy and fluorescence [32–35]. Wang et al. [36] quickly prepared N-doped CDs using citrate as C source and urea as N source by microwave procedure, and 8–270 nm oL/LK+ was determined by Aptresonance Rayleigh scatteringmethod. Chen et al. [37] put forward a colorimetry for detectionof 5 nmol/L K+, because ofthe conversion of anti-K+ Apt into a tersestrictG-quartet structure thatcann’taegisAuNPs from the change of color caused by K+ induced aggregation. Ma et al. [38] established a simple and uniform fluorescencemethodtodetect 5.0 × 10−4 mol/L K+, dued toAptlabeled by pyrene groups. In this paper, a microwave heating method was used to prepare N, Au-doped CDs with strong catalysis, anda newfluorescence/absorptionmethod forselective detection of ultratrace K+ wasbuiltby means of nanozyme and DNAzymeco-catalytic reactions.

Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Corporation Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) wasused toacquire fluorescence spectrawith voltage of 350 V, excited slit = emission slit of 10 nm and excitation wavelength of 280 nm; TU-1901 double beam UV–visible spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkingje General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) wasused to acquire absorption spectra; WX-6000 microwave digester(PreeKem Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) was used for the preparation of carbon dots; HH-S2 electric-heated thermostatic water bath (Jintan Land Automation Instrument Factory Co., Ltd., Jintan, China) SYZ-550 Quartz sub-boiling distiller (Jiangsu Crystal Glass Instrument Factory Co., Ltd., Nanjing, China), pH meter (Mettler-Toledo Instruments Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) and KQ3200DB CNC ultrasonic cleaner (Kunshan Ultrasonic instruments Co., Ltd., ultrasonic electric power 150w, working frequency 40 KHz) were used in all reaction processes; JEM‐2100F field emission transmission electron microscope (Electronics Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan. With dot resolution of 0.19 nm, line resolution of 0.14 nm, acceleration voltage of 200 kV and tilt angle of 25 degree) was used for material characterization.

A 1%HAuCl4solution: 1 g HAuCl4(Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China) was dissolved in 100 mL water; 500 μmol/L 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, CAS54827-17–7, T818493-5 g, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., China): 0.012 g tetramethylbenzidinewas dissolved in 100 mL of ethanol solution (ethanol: water = 1: 1);0.1 mmol/L H2O2: 100 μL 30% H2O2was gradually diluted to 0.1 mmol/L. pH 4.5 Tris-HCl buffer: 500 µL 0.1 mol/L Trisand 505 µL 0.1 mol/L HClwere diluted to 10 mL with water; 100 µmol/L heme chloride (HM, CAS16009-13-5, HBO289-1 g, Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd., China): 0.00326 g heme chloride in 100 mL ethanol solution (ethanol: water = 1: 1); glucoseandurea (Xilong Science Co., Ltd., China) were used.A HM-TMB solution was mixed 80 mL 1 × 10−6 mmol/L HM, 100 mL 5 × 10−4 mol/L TMB and 80 mL 5.2 mmol/L Tris-HCl buffer.Corn starch: prepared from fresh corn (purchased from the market) after crushing, separating and drying. The reagents were of analytical reagent, and the water was ultrapure water.

Preparation of CDN carbon dot sol: A10 mg corn starch (CS) was heated and dissolved in 20 mL water, 0, 1, 2, 3 mL of ethylenediamine (EDA) wereadded respectively and 10, 9, 8, 7 mL of water after cooling. The solution was transparent and colorless. Then the solution was digested in microwave digester on 140 °C/5 atm for 10 mins. The product of the reaction is a yellow and clear solution.After dialyzed in a 3500 Da dialysis bag for 14 h, the CDN0 and CDN1-3 concentration was 13.64 mg/mL.

Preparation of CDN/Au1-3 carbon dot sol:1g glucose and 1 g urea weredissolved in water. Then 0, 300 µL, 600 µL 0.1% HAuCl4, 100 µL 1% HAuCl4 solutionwere added respectively and diluted it to 30 mL, the solution was light yellow. Then the solution was digested in microwave digester on 140 °C at 5 atm for 10 min. The product of the reaction is a yellow and clear solution. After dialyzed in a 3500 Da dialysis bag for 14 h, the CDN and CDN/Au1-3 concentration was 18.77 mg/mL.

Preparation of CDE carbon dot sol: 1.0 mLEDA solution was dissolved in deionized water to 30 mL, Then the solution was digested in microwave digester on 140 °C and 5 atm for 10 min. The product of the reaction is a yellow and clear solution. After dialyzed in a 3500 Da dialysis bag for 14 h, the CDE concentration was 13.31 mg/mL.

Preparation of CDN1/Au1-3 carbon dot sol: A 10 mg corn starch was heated and dissolved in 20 mL water, 1 mL EDA was added after cooling, and then 300 µL, 600 µL 0.1% HAuCl4, 100 µL, 300 µL 1% HAuCl4 solution were added respectively, and diluted it to 30 mL, and the solution was pale yellow. Then the solution was digested in microwave digester on 140 °C and 5 atm for 10 min. The product of the reaction is a yellow and clear solution. After dialyzed in a 3500 Da dialysis bag for 14 h, the CDN1/Au1-3 concentration was 3.64 mg/mL.

A suitable of CD, 25 µL 2.5 × 10−6 mmol/L Apt, different concentrations of K+, 280 µL HM-TMB, 120 µL 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L H2O2 solution were added in a 5 mL tube, then were diluted to 1.5 mL and react in the 60 °C water bath for25 min. After cool with ice water, the fluorescence intensity at 403 nm (F 403nm), theblankwithout K+(F 403nm)0 were recorded and ΔF = F 403nm–(F 403nm)0 was calculated.

Apt is a G-rich nucleotide sequence of guanine, which can combine with K+ and HM to produce a stable DNAzyme of G-quadruplex/HM with rich π-π stacking electron that enhance the redox electrons transfer of TMB-H2O2 reaction, and exhibited catalytic effect [39]. CDs is a small nanoparticle that rich in π electrons and contain carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino, and hydrophobic groups on the surface. Those π-electrons accelerate the redox electron transfer between TMB and H2O2toexhibit catalytic effect. In the buffer solution of Tris-HCl at pH4.3, Apt and CDs were bound together by intermolecular Coulombic, hydrogen bonding, and hydrophobic forces. ThenCDs were wrapped by Apt toform CD-Apt resultingthe fluorescence quenching due to the inbihition of catalysis. When K+ions werepresent, the CD-Apt reacted with K+to form G-quadruplex and releasedCD nanoenzyme tofurther formingG-quadruplex/HM DNAzyme with HM. The two enzymes cooperate to catalyze the fluorescence/absorption reaction of H2O2-TMB, which greatly enhances the fluorescence peak at 430 nm and absorption peak value at 563 nm. The main reactions as follows,
(1)
CD + Apt = CD - Apt
(2)
CD - Apt +
K
+
+ HM = CD + DNAzyme
(3)
(4)
(5)

For the Eqs. (3) and (4), the rate V1 = d[TMBOX1]/dt, V2 = d[TMBOX2]/dt. The totle rate V was the sum of V1 and V2.When the concentration of fluorescence substance was low, the intensity F was linear to the concntration [TMBOX], that is, F = kF[TMBOX].According to the kinetic method of fixed time procedure, similar to enzyme kinetic analysis, the catalytic fluorescence Eq. (8) was obtained, with a conctant KT = KF[H2O2]α[TMB]βt. In the Eq. (8), the [catalyst] = [Nanozyme] + [DNAzyme], [Nanozyme] = [CD], that was proportional to the [K+] from Eq. (2). The [DNAzyme] was also proportional to the [K+]. Thus, [catalyst] was also proportional to the [K+]. The Nanozyme and DNAzyme dicatalytic fluorescence analytical Eq. (9) was obtained, with a conctant KD. Based on this, a new dicatalysis fluorescence/absorption analysis method for the detection of ultratrace K+ was established (Fig. 1 ).
(6)
V =
V 1
+
V 2
= d
TMB OX
/ dt = K
catalyst

O 2

TMB
β
(7)
dF / dt =
K F
catalyst

K T
[ catalyst ]

K D

K
+
]

Excitation (EX), fluorescence (FL) and absorption spectra of carbon dots:The EX and FL spectra of CDs were examined. When the emission wavelength (λem) was 400 nmfor CDN, the excited peak was at 280 and 355 nm, and the excited peakat 280 nm gradually increases with the concentration increasing. With λem of 455 nm, CDN/Au generated excited peaks at 230, 280 and 370 nm, and the peakat 370 nm was gradually enhanced with the increase of concentration (Fig. S1A). Results show that CDE, CDN0, CDN1, CDN2, CDN3, CDN1/Au1, CDN1/Au2, CDN1/Au3with λem of 385, 500, 400, 400, 400,320,320 and320 nmproduced a strong excited peak at 320 (Fig. S1B), 388, 320, 321, 325 (Fig. S1C), 320, 320 and 320 nm respectively, and those excited wavelengths were selected for use. FL spectra indicated that CDN/Au, CDE, CDN1, CDN2, CDN3 andCDN1/Au2exhibited astrong fluorescence peak at 455 (Fig. S1A), 385 (Fig. S1B), 405, 390, 395 (Fig. S1C) and 388 nm respectively, and the FL peak intensity gradually increases with the increase of concentration. The absorption spectra of CDN, CDN/Au, CDE, CDN0, CDN1, CDN2, CDN1/Au1, CDN1/Au2 and CDN1/Au3 were investigated. It can be seen from the spectrathat the CD ultraviolet absorption peakswereat 213, 207, 215, 215, 202, 206, 205, 201 and 208 nm respectively. The peak valueenhanceddue totheir concentration increased in the system.

Infrared (IR) spectra:A 10 mL of 18.77 mg/mL CDN,CDN/Au1,10 mL 13.31 mg/mL CDE, 10 mL 13.64 mg/mL CDN0,CDN1,CDN1/Au2were vacuum freeze dried at 0.1 Pa for 24 h. The solid samples were mixed with KBr and pressed into thin slices, respective. KBr was used as a reference. IR spectra show that, CDN (Fig. 2 D) exhibited a strong IR spectral peak at 3371 cm−1 for O–H stretching vibration (SV); 2127 and 1669 cm−1 for C = C SV of conjugate structure; 1454 cm−1 for CH2 shear vibration; 1407 and 1349 cm−1 for C–H out-of-plane swing; 1229 cm−1 for N = N = N SV of conjugate structure; 1161 cm−1 for in-plane bending; 1057 cm−1 for C-O-C antisymmetric stretching vibration; 900 cm−1 for ring vibration (RV); 783, 742 and 697 cm−1 for C–H out-of-plane bending; 592 cm−1 for C = C–H bending vibration (BV); 539 cm−1 for N-C = O bending vibration, which proved the success of doping CDs with N. For the CDN/Au1 (Fig. S3A), a strong infrared spectral peak appeared at 3379 cm−1 for C = O SV; 2345 cm−1 for N–H SV; 2129 cm−1 for C = N SV; 1609 cm−1 for C = C SV of conjugate structure; 1452 cm−1 for CH2 shear vibration; 1402 and 1068 cm−1 for C-O stretching vibration; 900 cm−1 forRV; 744 and 696 cm−1 for C–H out-of-plane bending; 621 cm−1 for C = C–H out-of-plane bending; 531 cm−1 for N-C = O bending vibration, which proved the success of doping CDs with N. For the CDE (Fig. S3B), the strong IR spectral peak appeared at 3367 and 2345 cm−1 for NH SV; 2130 cm−1 for N = N SV of conjugate structure; 1574 cm−1 for N–H deformation vibration; 1485 cm−1 for C = C SV of conjugate structure; 1384 cm−1 for C–H symmetric vibration; 1328 cm−1 for N–H deformation vibration; 1158 cm−1 for C–H out-of-plane swing; 1050 cm−1 for C-O SV; 956, 822 and 705 cm−1 for the C–H out-of-plane bending; 591 cm−1 for C = C–H bending vibration. For CDN0 (Fig. S3C), the strong IR spectral peaks appeared at 3431, 1633, 1384 and 1154 cm−1 for C = O SV; 2078 and 1485 cm−1 for C = C SV of conjugate structure; 1406 and 1332 cm−1 for C–H symmetric deformation vibration; 1025 cm−1 for C–H in-plane bending; 573 cm−1 for C = C–H bending vibration. For the CDN1 (Fig. S3D), the infrared spectral peaks appeared at 3380 cm−1 for O–H SV; 2345 cm−1 for N–H SV; 2138 cm−1 for C = N–H SV; 1575 and 1486 cm−1 for C = C SV of conjugate structure; 1384 cm−1 for C–H SV; 1330, 1156 and 1048 cm−1 for C = O SV; 954, 822 and 697 cm−1 for C–H out-of-plane bending; 589 cm−1 for C = C–H bending vibration, which proved the success of doping CDs with N. For the CDN1/Au2 (Fig. S3E), the strong IR peaks appeared at 3347 cm−1 for O–H SV; 2132, 1486 and 1614 cm−1 for C = C SV of conjugate structure; 1574 cm−1 for NH2 bending vibration; 1384 and 1329 cm−1 for C–H symmetric bending vibration; 1156 and 1047 cm−1 for C-O SV; 905, 822 and 707 cm−1 for C–H out-of-plane bending vibration; 589 cm−1 for C = C–H bending vibration, which proved the CDs was dopedwith N.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and absorption spectra of nanocatalytic system
ce:section.ce:para: In the catalytic system, due to the low concentration of CDs, itsfluorescence is very weak and can be ignored, comparing to the catalytic oxidation product TMBOXwith strong fluorescence. A280 nm was used as λex, TMBOXexhibited a strong fluorescence peak at 403 nm. In the N/Au-doped CD catalytic systems of CDN/CDN/Au1/CDN/Au2/CDN/Au3-H2O2-TMB (Fig. 4 A, Fig. S4A–C), the fluorescence peak intensity increased with the increase of carbon dots concentration, and the CDN/Au1 has the strongest catalysis. In the N-doped catalytic systems of CDE/CDN0/CDN1/CDN2-H2O2-TMB (Fig. 4B, Fig. S4D–F), the FL peak intensity increased with the carbon dots concentrationincreasing. The catalysis of CDN1/Au2 is the most sensitive. In the other N/Au-dopedcatalytic systems of CDN1/Au1/CDN1/Au2/CDN1/Au3-H2O2-TMB (Fig. 4C, Fig. S4G,H), the fluorescence peak intensity gradually increased with the increase of carbon dots concentration. the catalysis of CDN1/Au2 is the most sensitive, so this carbon dot was selected for used. In the three nanocatalysis analytical systems of CDN1/CDN/Au1/CDN1/Au2-H2O2-TMB-HM-Apt-K+, with λexof 320, 280 and 320 nm, a fluorescence peak was generated at 403 nm.When the K+concentration was increased, the FL peakintensity gradually increased (Fig. 4D–F). Among them, the CDN1/Au2-H2O2-TMB-HM-Apt-K+ system was the most sensitive, so it was selected to determine K+.

The absorption spectra ofCDN1/Au2/CDN/Au1/CDN1-TMB-H2O2-HM-Apt-KCl nanocatalytic analysis system were examined (Fig. S5). These three systems have a new absorption peak at about 563, 547 and 535 nm respectively, in addition the peak absorbance increased with the increase of KCl concentration. Among them, CDN1/Au2-TMB-H2O2-HM-Apt-KCl nanocatalysis analysis system has the highest sensitive. The absorption at 463 nm was chosen for used due to linearrangewith wide (0.02–1.5 µmol/LK+).In addition, the decreased absorption value at 435 nm also was linear to KCl concentration, but its interferences was inferior to the absorption method, due to many organic compouds containing benzene ring being of absorption near atultraviolet region. Although the linear relationship and detection limit of the absorption method were inferior to the fluorescence method, the absorption method was very low-cost.

Some precursors such as citrate, glucose, starch, natural plants including corn and spinach were used to prepare CDs [42,43], that exhibits catalysis of TMB-H2O2reaction due to the benzene ring structure containing π-π stacking electrons, and those electrons can ehance the redox electron transfer between TMB and H2O2. Inthe presence of doped N compound ethylenediamine (EDA), the corn starch (CS) taken place Maillardreaction to form Ndoped CD (CDN), under the microwave irradiation. The CDN exhibit strong catalysis. Furthermore, chloroauric acid was also added, the three compounds were irradiated to produce N/Au codoped CD with very strong catalysis.

Based on the catalysis of CD N1/Au2 , DNAzyme and the dienzyme of CD N1/Au2 -DNAzyme on the reaction, the kinetic parameters of three catalytic systems were studied by the fluorescence spectrophotometry according to a fixed time method, and then the concentration of one substrate was fixed to change the concentration of another substrate for kinetic experiments [44].According to the Michaelis-Menten equation of 1/V = Km/V m × 1/[S] + 1/V m, [S] was the substrate concentration,υ is the reaction rate that was proportional to the fluorescence intensity of TMBOX product,V mwas the maximum rate, K mwas the Michaelis constant. According to the [S] and υ obtained from the experiment, the working curve was drawn based on 1/V and 1/[S], and the catalytic kinetic parameters K m and V m were calculated by Lineweaver-Burk plot. The K mfor CD N1/Au2 , DNAzyme and the dienzyme was 0.065, 0.012 and 0.0052 mmol L−1, V mfor CD N1/Au2 , DNAzyme and the dienzymewas 4.02 × 10−8, 1.10 × 10−8 and 7.42 × 10−9 mol L−1 s−1 respectively. Results shows that the K m for the dienzymewas smallest, toexhibit best affinity for the substrate, and also to show best catalysis.

HM, G-quadruplex of Apt-K and G-quadruplex/HM exhibit catalysis of the fluorescence reaction between TMB and H2O2 due to the π-π stacking electron. The π-π stacking electrons of HM and G-quadruplex were weaker than the DNAzyme, and both catalytic activities were weaker than the DNAenzyme. TheDNAenzyme strategy has been utilized to determine potassium ions with low sensitivity [44]. In absence of catalyst, the redox electron transfer was very difficult between TMB and H2O2, that is, the redox reaction was very slow. In the dicatalytic analytical system, the both TMB and H2O2 were adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst. TheCDNAu contains rich electron Au atom and donor electron N atom, the surface electrons of CDNAu were higher than the orther CDs including CD and CDN, that strongly enhanced the redox electron stransfer. Meanwhile, the formed DNAzyme also enhanced the redox electron stransfer. Thus, the both DNAzyme and nanozyme showed very strong synergistic catalytic effect on this reaction of TMB-H2O2 to form TMBOX (Fig. 5 ).

The effects of Tris-HCl buffer pH, HM, Apt, TMB, H2O2, CDN1/Au2 concentration, the temperature, reaction time on the fluorescence signal were examined. As shown in results (Fig. S6), when the Tris-HCl buffer solution with a pH of 4.3 and concentration of 0.28 mmol/L, 50 nmol/L HM, 42 nmol/L Apt, 3.3 µmol/L TMB, 8 mmol/L H2O2, and 15 µg/mL CDN1/Au2 were added at 60 °C water bath temperature with 25 min, the fluorescence was strongest, and above conditions wre selected for use.

According to the procedure, the interference of coexisting substances (CS) on CDN1/Au2-H2O2-TMB-HM-Apt-K+ fluorescence determination of 0.5 nmol/L K+ was investigated. The results showed that the related error was within ± 10%,1000 times Li+ and Na+; 100 time Zn2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Ba2+,Ca2+,Cu2+, Cr3+,Al3+, HCO3 −, IO3 −, Br−, NH4 +, glycine, glutamic, aspartic acid and tyrosine; 50 times CO3 2−, NO2 −, Mg2+, phenylalanine and HAS; 10 times I− and Hg2+ don’t interfere with the FL determination (Table S1). These results demonstrated that this new fluorescence catalytic method has good selectivity.

Under the selected conditions in the experiment, the relationship of the analyte K+concnetration and the FL intensity ΔF403nm. The linear range, regress equation, coefficientand detection limitof CDN1/CDN/Au1/CDN1/Au2-H2O2-TMB-Apt-K+systems were 10–130, 10–270 and 0.06–1.3 nmol/L; ΔF403nm = 23.9C + 23.5, ΔF403nm = 15.7C + 63.6 and ΔF403nm = 690.9C + 64.4, 0.9844, 0.9495 and 0.9866; 5, 5 and of 0.03 nmol/L respectively. Among the three fluorescent systems, the CDN1/Au2 system was the most sensitive and was used in analysis of K+.Compared with some reportedaptamermolecular spectral methods (Table S2) [36,39,45,46], this catalytic FL method was one of the most sensitive analytical methods.

K+ is an important metal ion to maintain the physiological activities of cells in the body. It is involved in maintaining the normal permeability and acid-base balance of the body, participating in the metabolism of sugar and protein, and ensuring the normal function of neuromuscular. Highly sensitive method for K+can reduce blood consumption. The four sera were gained from the hospital, then it was diluted 1000 times with water to obtain a sample solution. According to the procedure, a 500 μL sample solution was measured in parallel five times, and the sample was subjected to a spike recovery test. The results were obtained (Table S3), the FL analytical results were agreement to the reference results. The recovery rate is 90–109%, and the relative standard deviation is 1.0–7.5%. This indicated that the new catalytic FL method wasreliable andaccurate.

In this article, high stability and highly catalytic active N,Au-codoped carbon dots were synthesized by microwaveirradiation procedure, and the CDN/Auexhibits the highest catalysis of TMB-H2O2 fluorescence/absorption indicator reaction. The aptamer wraps the carbon dots to reduce the fluorescence intensity due to inhibit the catalysis. When a certain concentration of K+was added, the specific combination of K+ and the aptamer forms a stable G-quadruplexstructure, and it further reacted with HM to form DNAzyme, meanwhile the CDs were releasedto restore its nanocatalysis. The π-π stacking electrons of both nanozyme and DNAzyme accelerated greatly the redox electron transfer, and co-catalyzed the fluorescence/colordreaction to enhance fluorescence/absorption signals greatly. On this basis, a new Apt mediated dicatalytic fluorescence/absorptiondual mode methodwas porposed, the linear range was 0.06–1.3 nmol/L, detection limit was 0.024 nmol/L, which has the advantages of ultra-high sensitivity and good selectivity. This newstrategy was also utilized to other aptamer reaction that formed DNAzyme of G-quadruplex and HM.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105508.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
